facts.claim: The dome's shutter stands open to the freezing sky.
facts.quote: The dome's shutter was open.

facts.claim: Hale distrusts a thing that arranges itself too neatly into an accident.
facts.quote: to distrust a thing that arranged itself too neatly into an accident

facts.claim: Crane lies dead at the foot of the iron stair, found at first light.
facts.quote: We found him at the foot of it, beneath the great glass, at first light.

facts.claim: The bruise is on the inner wrist, a band as if something closed there.
facts.quote: This was on the inner wrist, a band of it, as though something had closed there.

facts.claim: The meridian clock is stopped at fourteen minutes past three.
facts.quote: Its hands stood at fourteen minutes past three.

facts.claim: The clock was wound the evening before and had run only some six hours.
facts.quote: Then it had run some six hours and stopped.

facts.claim: Hale judges the clock was stopped by a human hand, not by the fall.
facts.quote: A clock stops because someone stops it ... This work is not fouled.

facts.claim: Pike drained the instrument account over three years into his own debts.
facts.quote: Pike had drained it, a little and a little, over three years, into debts of his own

facts.claim: Pike forged a telegram under the patron's name recalling Crane to dismissal.
facts.quote: He had forged a telegram, under the patron's name, recalling Crane to the city

facts.claim: The forged telegram lay undelivered in a drawer; Pike lost his nerve.
facts.quote: The telegram lay in a drawer; Pike had lost his nerve and not yet delivered it.

facts.claim: Crane never knew of the recall.
facts.quote: Crane never knew.

facts.claim: Pike came to believe Crane had read his dismissal and acted on that belief.
facts.quote: had come to believe, on the night Crane died, that Crane knew

facts.claim: Junia claims she was at the spectroscope in the annexe from eleven until dawn.
facts.quote: I was at the spectroscope ... From eleven until dawn.

facts.claim: Junia was on the dark gallery, not the annexe, and saw the whole of it.
facts.quote: She had been above, on the dark gallery ... and she had seen the whole of it.

facts.claim: Every plate is blank, unexposed; the camera was never opened to the sky.
facts.quote: Every plate was unexposed. ... It had not been opened to the sky.

facts.claim: Hale concludes either the camera failed unnoticed or Junia was not at it.
facts.quote: or you were not at your camera at all

facts.claim: The instrument account figures balance but are false; the regrinding fund is gone.
facts.quote: each entry true and the sum of them a lie ... the provision was gone

facts.claim: Hale knows none of three secrets: the wrong-hand log, the lost spare key, the unsent letter.
facts.quote: He did not know ... a spare dome key, reported lost a month past ... a letter, never sent

facts.claim: A spare dome key was reported lost a month past and lies where it should not.
facts.quote: a spare dome key, reported lost a month past, lay even now where it should not lie

facts.claim: Somewhere is a letter, never sent, in which Crane resigned his own post.
facts.quote: a letter, never sent, in which Aurel Crane resigned his own post in his own hand

facts.claim: Pike's defence is that Crane knew and approved the drawing-down.
facts.quote: The director knew. Crane knew, and approved it

facts.claim: Eight hundred and forty pounds has left the account under the regrinding heading.
facts.quote: Eight hundred and forty pounds has left this account under that heading.

facts.claim: Pike lets slip that the recall came and Crane read it and knew he was finished.
facts.quote: The recall came, and he read it, and he knew he was finished

facts.claim: Hale marks the direction of Pike's frightened glance toward the study drawer.
facts.quote: marked the direction of a frightened man's glance

facts.claim: The night-log's final entry is in Junia's looser hand, not Crane's.
facts.quote: Junia had written the night-log ... in her hand

facts.claim: Hale sees Junia manufactured a record of work the blank plates prove undone.
facts.quote: She had manufactured a record of work that the blank plates proved had never been done.

facts.claim: The lost spare key is found behind the meridian clock on the gallery.
facts.quote: found now behind the very clock that a hand had stopped at three-fourteen

facts.claim: Hale infers someone carried the lost key up to the gallery.
facts.quote: Someone had carried the lost key up to this gallery.

facts.claim: Junia weighs whether to tell or bury; she means to shield someone.
facts.quote: She held the truth ... And she held, still, the means to bury it.

facts.claim: Crane's signed resignation, dated three days before death, is found unsent in the drawer.
facts.quote: It was dated three days before his death. It was complete. It was signed. And it had never been sent.

facts.claim: Hale sees a man pushed toward a door he had already opened himself.
facts.quote: to push a man toward a door he had already opened himself

facts.claim: Hale theorizes Junia shields someone who was there; he will have it within the day.
facts.quote: I think you are shielding someone who was there.

facts.claim: The unsent letter takes a prop from under Junia's justification for silence.
facts.quote: the letter had taken some prop from under it

facts.claim: Junia stands at the hinge: speak the name or burn the night-log.
facts.quote: Or she could end it the other way: feed the night-log to the fire

facts.claim: Hale gives Pike a mild good-night, leaving him believing himself uncaught.
facts.quote: Good night to you.

facts.claim: Toward two, Pike goes soft-footed by Hale's door to the director's study.
facts.quote: went by Hale's door with a shielded light, toward the director's study

facts.claim: Pike reads the telegram, does not destroy it, puts it back and presses on the drawer.
facts.quote: He did not destroy it. ... he put it back in the drawer ... pressed his hand flat on the closed drawer

facts.claim: Hale reads Pike's fear as larger and older, with a death in it.
facts.quote: it was larger and older and had a death in it

facts.claim: Hale finds the night-log's last entry in Junia's looser hand under an honest reading.
facts.quote: The assistant had written the final night-log ... in her own hand

facts.claim: A thread of cloth is caught in the clock works where a hand reached to still it.
facts.quote: a thread of cloth caught on the rough cast iron of the works

facts.claim: The lost spare key is found behind the stopped clock on the gallery.
facts.quote: Lying behind the very clock a hand had stopped at three-fourteen.

facts.claim: By inventory pretext Hale opens the drawer and finds Crane's signed unsent resignation.
facts.quote: Aurel Crane's letter of resignation. ... Complete. Signed. Never sent.

facts.claim: Pike did not know Crane had already resigned of his own hand.
facts.quote: told Hale that he had not

facts.claim: Hale has everything but the name; the name lives behind Junia's eyes.
facts.quote: He had everything but the name, and the name lived behind Junia Frost's clear eyes.

facts.claim: The resignation does its quiet work; the prop goes out from under Junia.
facts.quote: He watched the prop go out from under her

facts.claim: By the quiet road Junia reaches the same hinge: speak the name or burn the log.
facts.quote: Or she could lean and feed the night-log to the low fire

facts.claim: Junia admits she was on the gallery, not the annexe; the camera never opened.
facts.quote: I was on the gallery. ... The camera never opened. That is why the plates are blank.

facts.claim: Junia names Pike as the man who climbed the iron stair at three.
facts.quote: The man on the stair was Onslow Pike.

facts.claim: Pike was certain Crane had read the recall and was going to ruin him.
facts.quote: Certain that Crane had read some recall, some dismissal

facts.claim: Crane was bewildered, asking what; he had never heard of any recall.
facts.quote: Read what, man? Known what?

facts.claim: They wrestled on the iron rail; Crane's foot went on the frost; Pike had the wrist.
facts.quote: Crane's foot went on the frost. Pike had his wrist

facts.claim: Pike caught the clock works to save himself, stopping the pendulum at 3:14.
facts.quote: stopped the pendulum dead with his own gripping hand. That is your clock at three-fourteen

facts.claim: Hale confronts Pike with the witness and that Crane had never seen the recall.
facts.quote: She saw you on the stair. ... He had read nothing. He had never seen it.

facts.claim: Pike breaks at the words 'he had never seen it' and asks if Crane did not know.
facts.quote: He didn't —? he said. He didn't know?

facts.claim: Hale certifies the death not an accident but something else, by human agency; policy will not pay.
facts.quote: not an accident within the meaning of the instrument policy ... a death by human agency

facts.claim: Pike spent a forgery and a death to drive out a man already walking through the door of his own will.
facts.quote: a man who was already, of his free and gentle will, walking through the door

facts.claim: Junia admits she was on the gallery, not the annexe; the camera never opened.
facts.quote: I was on the gallery. ... The camera never opened. That is why the plates are blank.

facts.claim: Junia names Pike as the man who climbed the iron stair at three.
facts.quote: The man on the stair was Onslow Pike.

facts.claim: Pike was certain Crane had read the recall and was going to ruin him.
facts.quote: Certain that Crane had read some recall, some dismissal

facts.claim: They wrestled on the iron rail; Crane's foot went on the frost; Pike had the wrist.
facts.quote: Crane's foot went on the frost. Pike had his wrist

facts.claim: Pike caught the clock works to save himself, stopping the pendulum at 3:14.
facts.quote: stopped the pendulum dead with his own gripping hand. That is your clock at three-fourteen

facts.claim: Pike keeps the forged telegram because it is his only proof the death had a cause.
facts.quote: That paper is the proof, to him, that there was a cause.

facts.claim: Junia shielded Pike because his self-punishment exceeds any court's, and judged.
facts.quote: I judged. I had no right to, and I judged.

facts.claim: Hale certifies not an accident, something else; policy will not pay; left to the slow law.
facts.quote: not an accident within the meaning of the policy ... The policy would not pay; the truth was something else.

facts.claim: Hale shows Pike that Crane never read the wire and had resigned of his own will.
facts.quote: He never knew, Mr. Pike. He never read your wire.

facts.claim: Junia feeds the night-log to the fire; the looser hand and the record are gone.
facts.quote: she leaned, and she fed it to the fire.

facts.claim: The name lives in memory alone; the book is only its shadow, now ash.
facts.quote: The name lived in memory alone. The book was only its shadow.

facts.claim: Hale can draw the whole figure but the centre, the single human act, is burned.
facts.quote: one hole exactly the size of a name

facts.claim: Hale certifies not an accident, something else, cause by human agency, agent unestablished.
facts.quote: something else, ... cause of death by human agency, agent unestablished

facts.claim: Hale certifies the provable irony: a wire forged to drive out a man already resigned, even without a name.
facts.quote: the whole apparatus of fraud aimed at a door the dead man had already, quietly, opened
